title: Water treatment and potable water
examTip: AQA tests the steps of water treatment — sedimentation, filtration, chlorination — in order. Also know what 'potable water' means (safe to drink, not necessarily pure), and why sea water requires a different (more expensive) process (desalination). Distinguish pure water (only H₂O) from potable water.
introTips: Potable water is not the same as pure water. Pure water contains only H₂O. Potable water is safe to drink but still contains dissolved minerals.
Learn the three stages of drinking water treatment in order: sedimentation → filtration → chlorination.
Desalination (removing salt from sea water) is mentioned in AQA but is noted as expensive and energy-intensive — know why.
intro: Access to safe drinking water is one of the greatest public health achievements in history. In the UK, water from rivers and reservoirs goes through a multi-stage treatment process before reaching your tap. Understanding how this works — and why different water sources need different treatment — is the key C10 chemistry you need.

keyPoints.heading: Potable water — safe to drink, not necessarily pure
keyPoints.content: Potable water is water that is safe to drink. It is not the same as pure water. Pure water contains only H₂O molecules — no dissolved substances at all. Potable water has low levels of dissolved minerals and salts (which are harmless or even beneficial), no harmful microorganisms, and no toxic chemicals. In the UK, potable water comes from: surface water (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater (underground aquifers). Fresh water sources have much lower dissolved salt content than sea water, making treatment simpler and cheaper.
writeBullets: Potable water = safe to drink — NOT the same as pure water
Pure water = only H₂O (no dissolved substances)
Potable water: low dissolved minerals, no harmful microorganisms, no toxic chemicals
UK sources: surface water (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater (aquifers)
Fresh water is easier and cheaper to treat than sea water

keyTerms.def: Water that is safe to drink — has low dissolved mineral levels and no harmful microorganisms; not necessarily chemically pure
Water containing only H₂O molecules — no dissolved substances; not the same as potable water
Water that has collected in underground rock layers (aquifers), a source of potable water

keyPoints.memTip: Potable ≠ pure. Pure = H₂O only. Potable = safe to drink (has minerals dissolved in it, just no harmful ones). You drink potable water every day — it's not pure. The water from a chemistry lab tap would be pure, but that's not what you want to drink.

keyPoints.heading: Stages of water treatment
keyPoints.content: Fresh water from rivers and reservoirs is treated in three main stages before it reaches the tap. (1) Sedimentation: the water is held in large settlement tanks. Chemicals (e.g. aluminium sulfate) may be added to cause particles to clump together and sink (flocculation). Large solid particles settle to the bottom. (2) Filtration: the water passes through layers of sand and gravel, which remove smaller suspended particles that didn't settle. (3) Chlorination: a small, controlled amount of chlorine is added to kill any remaining harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses). Chlorine is carefully dosed — enough to kill pathogens but not enough to be harmful to humans.
writeBullets: Stage 1 — Sedimentation: particles settle in tanks; flocculation chemicals may help clumping
Stage 2 — Filtration: water passes through sand/gravel layers → removes suspended particles
Stage 3 — Chlorination: chlorine added → kills harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses)
Chlorine carefully dosed — kills pathogens without harming humans

keyTerms.def: The process of allowing particles to settle out of water in large tanks — the first stage of water treatment
Passing water through sand and gravel layers to remove smaller suspended particles — the second stage of water treatment
Adding controlled amounts of chlorine to water to kill harmful microorganisms — the final stage of water treatment

keyPoints.memTip: Three stages in order: Sediment → Filter → Chlorinate. SFC. Sediment out big particles, filter out small ones, chlorine kills the microorganisms. Remember: chlorination is the biological safety step — the first two are physical.

keyPoints.heading: Desalination — treating sea water
keyPoints.content: Sea water contains far too much dissolved salt to drink — it cannot be treated by simple sedimentation and filtration. To make sea water potable, the salt must be removed by desalination. Two main methods: (1) Distillation — sea water is heated until it evaporates, and the water vapour is collected and condensed, leaving the salt behind. The condensed water is pure. (2) Reverse osmosis — sea water is forced under very high pressure through a membrane that allows water molecules through but blocks salt ions. Both methods are expensive and energy-intensive compared to treating fresh water. In water-scarce countries (e.g. Saudi Arabia, UAE), desalination is essential despite the cost.
writeBullets: Sea water has too much dissolved salt for normal treatment
Desalination methods: distillation (evaporate water, condense) OR reverse osmosis (high-pressure membrane)
Both methods are expensive and energy-intensive
Distillation: heat water → evaporate → condense → pure water collected
Reverse osmosis: high pressure forces water through a semi-permeable membrane
Used in water-scarce regions where fresh water sources are unavailable

keyTerms.def: The process of removing dissolved salts from sea water to produce potable water — by distillation or reverse osmosis
A desalination method that forces sea water through a semi-permeable membrane under high pressure, separating pure water from dissolved salts
Separation of a liquid mixture by evaporation and condensation — used in desalination to separate pure water from salt

keyPoints.memTip: Desalination = de-salt-ination. Two methods: Distillation (boil, collect steam) or Reverse osmosis (pressure through membrane). Both = expensive + energy-intensive. Normal water treatment is cheap because fresh water doesn't have much salt to start with.

tables.title: Water treatment — fresh water vs sea water

0.question: What is the correct order of the three main stages of water treatment?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — sedimentation first (large particles settle), then filtration (smaller particles removed through sand/gravel), then chlorination (microorganisms killed).
0.wrongFeedback: The correct order is: sedimentation → filtration → chlorination. Sedimentation removes large particles (they settle out). Filtration removes smaller suspended particles (through sand and gravel). Chlorination kills microorganisms. Each stage handles what the previous stage left behind.

1.question: Why is potable water NOT the same as pure water?
options: Potable water contains harmful microorganisms, while pure water does not
Potable water contains dissolved minerals and salts, while pure water contains only H₂O
Potable water comes from rivers, while pure water comes from the laboratory
Potable water has been treated with chlorine, which makes it chemically impure
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — potable water has dissolved minerals (low levels, harmless or beneficial), while pure water contains only H₂O. Safe to drink ≠ chemically pure.
1.wrongFeedback: Potable water is safe to drink but still contains dissolved minerals — it is not chemically pure. Pure water contains only H₂O molecules with nothing dissolved in it. The minerals in drinking water are at safe levels and don't make it harmful. Chlorine is also present at very low, carefully controlled levels.

commonMistakes: Saying potable water is pure water — potable means safe to drink, not chemically pure. Tap water has dissolved minerals and trace chlorine.

Getting the order of water treatment wrong — it's always: sedimentation (big particles settle) → filtration (smaller particles) → chlorination (kill microorganisms). Reversing any steps doesn't make sense.
Saying distillation removes all dissolved substances but reverse osmosis doesn't — both remove dissolved salts effectively; they're different methods, not different results.
Forgetting to say why desalination is expensive — it requires large amounts of energy (to heat water for distillation, or to maintain high pressure for reverse osmosis).
examTips: For water treatment questions, always give the stages IN ORDER and explain what each one removes. AQA often gives 3 marks — one for each stage.
Distinguish potable from pure: if asked 'is potable water pure?', the answer is no — give the reason (contains dissolved minerals).
For desalination evaluation questions: benefit = makes sea water drinkable (essential in water-scarce regions); limitation = expensive and energy-intensive (environmental and economic cost).
When asked about chlorination, include both what it does (kills microorganisms) AND the precaution (carefully dosed to avoid harming humans).
revisionCardBullets: Potable water = safe to drink — NOT the same as pure water (which is only H₂O)
Potable water contains dissolved minerals (low levels) and no harmful microorganisms
Sedimentation: particles settle in tanks (flocculation may help)
Filtration: sand and gravel layers remove remaining suspended particles
Chlorination: chlorine kills harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses)
Sea water: too salty for normal treatment → desalination needed
Desalination methods: distillation (heat and condense) or reverse osmosis (high-pressure membrane)
Desalination is expensive and energy-intensive compared to fresh water treatment
